title: The Hidden Independence Axiom
claim: Some acts are intrinsically evil — always wrong regardless of intention or circumstances.
hiddenAssumption: The moral character of an action's "object" can be evaluated independently of all context. The three-source framework (object + intention + circumstances) alone doesn't entail this — it only says all three matter. The independence of the object is an additional, unstated axiom.
surprise: The Catechism presents intrinsic evil as following from its moral framework, but it's actually an extra premise. The framework is compatible with context-dependent evaluation. The independence claim is doing invisible philosophical work — it's the deontological core of Catholic ethics, smuggled in without announcement.

title: The Binary Ontology of Sin
claim: Mortal sin requires grave matter, full knowledge, and complete consent. Venial sin is everything else. Mortal sin causes loss of grace.
hiddenAssumption: Five binary assumptions the text doesn't state: (1) knowledge is full or not-full — no spectrum, (2) consent is complete or not-complete — no spectrum, (3) matter is grave or less serious — no spectrum, (4) grace is present or absent — no spectrum, (5) mortal sin causally removes grace — a brute axiom connecting human acts to divine states with no stated mechanism.
surprise: The Catechism's moral theology rests on a deeply binary ontology. Everything is either/or. A graded model of knowledge, consent, or gravity would break the clean mortal/venial partition. The proof assistant forced us to choose: binary or graded? The Catechism chose binary without saying so.

title: The Libertarian Freedom Requirement
claim: Hell is 'definitive self-exclusion from communion with God' — the result of free choice, not divine predestination.
hiddenAssumption: Five unstated axioms: (1) Love requires freedom — why can't an unfree being love? (2) Libertarian free will — 'by our own free choice' assumes genuine alternatives existed, not just absence of coercion. (3) Death is final — no post-mortem change of will. (4) Mortal sin prevents love of God — mechanism unstated. (5) No predestination — hell is always self-chosen.
surprise: The 'self-exclusion' framing ONLY works under libertarian free will. Under compatibilism, the person acted from their desires but may not have been able to desire otherwise. The Catechism commits to libertarian freedom without announcing it. And the axiom that love requires freedom is genuinely surprising — the Catechism never explains WHY.

title: The Grace Bootstrapping Problem
claim: The preparation for grace is itself a work of grace. God's initiative demands man's free response.
hiddenAssumption: Three hidden premises: (1) Grace type hierarchy — at least two KINDS of grace (prevenient and sanctifying) must exist; without this, §2001 is circular. (2) Prevenient grace is unconditioned — God gives it without prior human action, breaking the infinite regress. (3) Divine initiative preserves freedom — God causes our willing while our willing remains free; mechanism unexplained.
surprise: §2001 contains a genuine bootstrapping problem: you need grace to prepare for grace. The Catechism compresses the entire Pelagian controversy — one of the longest-running debates in Christian theology (5th century to present) — into two paragraphs. The proof assistant forced it all back open. The need for a typed grace hierarchy was the most surprising finding.

title: The Trinity Requires a Different Logic
claim: One God in three persons. Each person is God whole and entire. The persons are really distinct. The distinction is solely relational.
hiddenAssumption: Four hidden premises: (1) Identity is relative, not absolute — 'same God' does not entail 'same person,' contradicting Leibniz's law. (2) Persons ARE relations, not individuals that have relations. (3) Relational opposition is the sole source of distinction. (4) The divine substance is indivisible — non-standard mereology.
surprise: The Trinity is not illogical — it's logical in a different logic. The proof assistant refused to let us use standard equality for the Trinity. Under standard logic, if Father = God and Son = God, then Father = Son. The Catechism's resolution requires relative identity: 'same F' does not entail 'same G.' This is a non-standard logic that most people assume is just standard logic. The Catechism never announces the switch.
provenanceSource: CCC §253, §254, §255; Nicaea (325 AD); Fourth Lateran Council (1215)

title: The Rational Convergence Assumption
claim: Natural law is universal, immutable, eternal, and accessible to human reason. Yet its application varies greatly by place, time, and circumstances.
hiddenAssumption: Five hidden premises: (1) Moral realism — moral facts exist independently of human opinion. (2) Rational accessibility — reason alone discovers moral truth. (3) Rational convergence — if people disagree about morality, someone is reasoning badly (extraordinarily strong claim hidden in 'universal'). (4) Law/application distinction — the law is immutable even though applications vary (requires Platonic realism). (5) Divine grounding — natural law only binds because it comes from God, creating a tension with 'accessible to reason.'
surprise: Five major metaphysical commitments in four paragraphs, none stated explicitly. The rational convergence assumption was the most surprising: if natural law is universal AND accessible to reason, then persistent moral disagreement is always a failure of reason, never a feature of genuine complexity. Also: natural law is 'natural' (discoverable by reason) but requires God to have binding force — so an atheist can discover it but can't account for why it binds.

title: The Erring Conscience Paradox
claim: You must always obey your conscience. But your conscience can be wrong. If the error is your fault, you're culpable.
hiddenAssumption: Five hidden premises: (1) Two-level moral evaluation — act-goodness and agent-culpability can diverge. (2) Culpability asymmetry — acting AGAINST conscience is categorically worse than following an erring one. (3) Binary ignorance — culpable or innocent, no spectrum. (4) Duty to inquire — you must form your conscience properly. (5) Conscience is reason, not feeling.
surprise: The culpability asymmetry was the key finding. If your conscience tells you to do something evil: you're wrong if you follow it AND wrong if you don't. The resolution creates an unstated asymmetry — acting against conscience is always worse. This is the decision rule of Catholic moral epistemology, and the text never argues for it.

title: The Good/Evil Causation Asymmetry
claim: God guides all creation, operates 'in and through' all creature actions, but is 'in no way' the cause of moral evil. Creatures have genuine freedom.
hiddenAssumption: Six hidden premises: (1) Two-tier causation — God and creatures cause at different levels without competing. (2) Granted causation — creaturely causation is real but derived. (3) Foreknowledge-freedom compatibility — asserted without mechanism. (4) Good/evil causation asymmetry — God 'operates through' good but only 'permits' evil. (5) Permission is not causation. (6) Freedom is valuable enough to justify permitting evil.
surprise: God's relationship to causation changes based on the moral character of the effect. Good acts: God operates through them. Evil acts: God merely permits them. This asymmetry requires evil to be a privation (absence of good) rather than a positive reality — the privation theory of evil is doing invisible work. Six axioms in ten paragraphs, none stated.
provenanceSource: CCC §302-311; Aquinas ST I q.22-23, q.105

title: Your Soul Is Not What You Think It Is
claim: The soul is the 'form' of the body. Body and soul are not two natures united but form a single nature.
hiddenAssumption: Six hidden premises: (1) Aristotelian hylomorphism as the philosophical framework — adopted without naming it. (2) 'Form' is a real metaphysical principle, not a metaphor. (3) Anti-dualism — explicitly denies soul-in-body model that most people assume. (4) Anti-materialism — matter without soul is not a dead body, it's not a body at all. (5) Soul hierarchy — human souls are categorically different from animal souls. (6) Non-standard composition — the soul is not a PART of you but the PRINCIPLE of you.
surprise: Most people (including most Catholics) think soul/body = mind/body: two separate things joined together. The Catechism says something much more radical. The soul is what MAKES your body a body. Without it, the matter isn't a corpse — it's not a body at all. The Catechism's anthropology is incompatible with the mind-body dualism that most modern people assume. The proof assistant forced this specificity into the open.
provenanceSource: CCC §355-365; Aristotle, De Anima; Aquinas ST I q.75-76; Council of Vienne (1312)

title: The Perfect Freedom Paradox
claim: The more one does what is good, the freer one becomes. The choice to do evil is an abuse of freedom and leads to slavery.
hiddenAssumption: Six hidden premises: (1) Teleological freedom — freedom has a direction toward God, not neutral between options. (2) Freedom is graded — you become more or less free based on choices. (3) Evil is self-undermining — choosing evil reduces capacity for good. (4) Perfect freedom = inability to sin — the freest being cannot choose evil. (5) Proportional responsibility — graded freedom means graded responsibility. (6) Two concepts of freedom coexist without stated relationship.
surprise: Perfect freedom is the INABILITY to choose evil. Most people think freedom means having all options open. The Catechism says the opposite: the ability to choose evil is a deficiency of freedom, not its essence. God (who cannot sin) is the freest being. This is the most counterintuitive finding in the entire project — and the Catechism never flags that its concept of freedom is radically different from the modern one.

title: The Epistemic Gap in Self-Defense
claim: Self-defense is legitimate if force is proportionate. Defense can be a duty for those responsible for others' lives.
hiddenAssumption: Four hidden premises: (1) The full double effect doctrine has four conditions; the Catechism states only two. (2) Proportionality assumes the defender can accurately assess the threat — but real self-defense happens under fear and imperfect information. (3) Defense can be a DUTY, creating positive obligation to use violence. (4) The means/ends condition (unstated) prevents collapsing defense into assassination.
surprise: The epistemic gap: proportionality requires knowing how much force is 'necessary,' but self-defense happens in crisis conditions where threat assessment is unreliable. The Catechism never addresses what happens when the defender genuinely believed the threat was lethal but it wasn't. Also, two of the four standard double-effect conditions are invisible in the text.
provenanceSource: CCC §2263-2267; Aquinas ST II-II q.64 a.7

title: The Axiom Set IS the Denomination
claim: Grace justifies by transforming the person internally. Justification involves human cooperation enabled by grace. It is conferred in Baptism.
hiddenAssumption: Four hidden premises: (1) Grace is transformative, not just forensic — it actually changes you, not just your legal status. (2) Human cooperation in justification is real but grace-enabled (same bootstrapping as §2001). (3) Baptism is causally connected to justification — a sacramental axiom linking salvation to the Church. (4) Justification is a process, not a single event.
surprise: The Catholic-Protestant disagreement on justification can be formalized as a difference in axiom sets. The Protestant position is obtained by replacing all four Catholic axioms: transformative → forensic, cooperation → reception, baptism → faith alone, process → event. The denominational difference IS the axiom-set difference. This confirms the project's central thesis: the axiom set is the theological tradition.
provenanceSource: CCC §1987-1993; Council of Trent Session 6 (1547); Rom 3:22
